$HTZ $HTZ Market's pricing a broken rental car company and missing the option.  Hertz's problem isn't depreciation — it beats $CAR on DPU. It's utilization: the cars sit idle. One buyer can fix that → $UBER. 40M trips/day, a $10B AV program, and no fleet-ops backbone. Rent/ride/sell flex turns idle cars into revenue, and Hertz is the ready-made national robotaxi fleet layer Waymo/Amazon/Tesla all lack.  A strategic buyer could justify ~$7 (vs ~$3-4 tape) and still get paid back in ~2-3 yrs, accretive year one.  But: neg book equity, 2028 maturity wall, dilution, and a deal may never come. Speculative optionality — not a promise. 🎲

$HTZ $CAR $LYFT $GRAB $UBER    Hertz's recent major financial crisis was primarily caused by its massive, failed bet on Tesla. In 2021, announced it would buy 100,000 Teslas to electrify its fleet, but the move resulted in billions of dollars in losses that ultimately forced out its CEO and triggered a massive sell-off of these cars.  The key factors behind this failure were: 1. Elon Musk's "Depreciation Trap".  Starting in 2023, Tesla aggressively slashed prices on its new models to stay competitive. This instantly tanked the resale value of Hertz's used fleet. 2. Sky-High Repair Costs Hertz reported that its Teslas were getting into collisions more frequently repairing them was exceptionally expensive. 3. Lack of customer interest The actual rental demand for these vehicles was much lower than projected.  Hertz canceled its ambitious electrification goals to stabilize its finances, began liquidating more than 20,000 Teslas (below $20,000) and shifted back to internal combustion cars

$UBER $LYFT Stock futures moved sharply lower Wednesday after U.S. President Donald Trump told the NATO summit in Turkey that the ceasefire with Iran is over amid renewed hostilities in the Middle East that sent oil prices surging Futures on the Dow Jones Industrial Average were down 476 points, or 0.9%, following Trump's comments in Ankara. Futures tied to the S&P 500 were 0.7% lower, and Nasdaq 100 futures dropped 1%. Trump's remarks followed what the U.S. called a series of powerful strikes against Iran on Tuesday in retaliation to attacks against three commercial vessels traveling in the Strait of Hormuz. Speaking to reporters at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday, the military alliance's Secretary General Mark Rutte said America's strikes were "absolutely necessary” "When you have a ceasefire and Iran is basically violating the ceasefire — we see what happened yesterday with ships being attacked — I think it is totally crucial that the U.S. forcefully reacts," he said

$LCID I’d like to blame retail for this.  Call $LCID bulls weak.  But the truth is this is mathmatical mechanics.  All the shares are owned between $UBER, $BAC and PIF.  $BAC is the leverage and PIF is the flywheel.  On days like this it doesn’t matter how bullish you are $BAC makes money from lending shares and killing calls/puts.  So they keep it channeled.  On the ultra lows the PIF Hoovers up shares slowly but surely gaining ownership while making money.  I had thought with this last run they were done and were going to let it go.  Maybe if market forces were stronger.  But on a day like today it’s just easy money for them to borrow out shares to short.  You’ll know this is going to move when those available shares dissapear and the borrow rate goes up.  Until then retail is lunch for $BAC

$GOOGL $DASH $UBER    Google may be quietly expanding Gemini deeper into everyday consumer behavior.    Reports suggest Google Maps is working on an AI-powered feature that could let users discover restaurants and place food orders directly inside the app.    If rolled out at scale, this could reshape how users interact with local search and potentially create more pressure on existing delivery platforms.    The bigger story is AI moving from a chatbot layer into real-world transactions. Whoever controls the discovery layer has a chance to capture more value from the ecosystem.    Still early, but worth watching how this develops.

$UBER options setup is basically a volatility + patience game here.    Stock is around $74.35 (July 4, 2026 close), still sitting roughly -9% YTD, and headlines like the Waymo Phoenix partnership ending just add short term noise more than structural change.    Two clean income-style structures here:    Cash secured put  Sell puts below current price, collect premium upfront. If price stays above strike, premium is kept. If it breaks below, assignment happens and shares are acquired at an effective discount.    Covered call  If already holding shares, selling calls above current price generates income while waiting. Upside gets capped if price runs through strike, but premium cushions downside.    Neither is free money. Sharp downside moves still hurt, and strong upside will always come with opportunity cost.    Key question here  Do you want to own UBER on dips, or just monetize range behavior while the market decides direction    Curious how others are structuring this name here

Interesting value gap forming here.    An $UBER ride to the Hamptons used to be around $200. Now it’s pushing ~$600 depending on timing and traffic… and you’re still sitting in 3–5 hours of highway congestion.    Meanwhile, a Blade helicopter ride used to be $2,000+. Now it’s down to roughly $965 for ~30 minutes in the air, with a completely different experience.    That gap between “ground inefficiency” and “air mobility premium” is starting to compress fast.    And with Blade now effectively sitting under $JOBY, you can see where this is heading as eVTOL scales over the next few years.    Curious how pricing dynamics evolve once air mobility starts competing at scale 😎    I often get asked why I don’t turn this into paid content, but for me, sharing stock information is just a hobby. I’m not financially struggling, so I choose to share it for free.
